anchor.text: the dashboard timeline slipped due to an unexpected API redesign in May

messages.author: ai
messages.text: What caused the 'unexpected' redesign? Was it external (upstream API change) or internal (scope creep)? The word 'unexpected' deflects accountability — consider being more specific about the root cause.

anchor.text: We have no fallback plan for this scenario

messages.author: ai
messages.text: This is a critical admission buried in the middle of the doc. A dependency with no fallback and a hard deadline should be called out as a top-level risk, not mentioned in passing. What's the mitigation if payments team slips?

anchor.text: the security review has not been scheduled yet

messages.author: ai
messages.text: Who owns scheduling this? 'Has not been scheduled' is passive voice hiding accountability. Add an owner and a deadline, or this will still be unscheduled when it becomes a blocker.

anchor.text: Integration testing is 80% done with no blockers identified so far

messages.author: ai
messages.text: 80% done with no blockers is suspiciously optimistic. What's the remaining 20%? Is it the hard parts or easy cleanup? 'No blockers so far' is a red flag — what categories of testing remain?

messages.author: human
messages.text: Fair point. The remaining 20% is the payments integration (which depends on v3 API) and the batch export edge cases. So yes, the hard parts are left.

messages.author: ai
messages.text: So the 80% figure is misleading — it's really 'we've tested everything except the risky parts.' Consider rephrasing to '80% of test cases pass; remaining 20% depend on the payments v3 API delivery.' That sets expectations correctly.

messages.author: human
messages.text: But teh thing is i am not happy

anchor.contextBefore: track for a July release. The
